title_en: Contracts and Marriage in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i
title_ar: العقود والنكاح في بدائع الصنائع في ترتيب الشرائع
content_en:
The commercial and family law sections of Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i demonstrate the full power of al-Kasani's systematic method in the areas where Islamic law is most practically consequential. His conditions-pillars-void/defective distinction framework transforms complex areas of commercial and personal status law into navigable systems.

For the contract of sale, al-Kasani's framework identifies: the conditions for validity (shurut as-sihha) — legal capacity, existing and owned subject matter, deliverability, specified price, absence of conditions that invalidate the contract; the pillars (arkan) — the offer (ijab) and acceptance (qabul); and the invalidating factors that render a contract void (batil, lacking a pillar) or defective (fasid, meeting the pillars but violating a condition). The void/defective (batil/fasid) distinction is among the most important features of Hanafi commercial law and is given particular clarity by al-Kasani's systematic presentation.

The defective (fasid) contract in Hanafi law — valid as to its pillars but invalid as to some condition — occupies a legal space that other schools do not recognize in the same form. Al-Kasani explains that a fasid contract, if executed, transfers ownership but creates a sin and an obligation to cure the defect. This nuanced treatment of defective contracts reflects the Hanafi school's sophistication in commercial law.

The sections on riba in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i apply al-Kasani's framework to identify which transactions are void and which are merely defective due to riba. Explicit riba — exchange of gold for gold with excess — renders the contract void (batil). Disguised riba through a fasid structure — for example, a sale with a condition that introduces prohibited interest — renders it defective (fasid). This distinction has practical implications for remedies and for the legal treatment of parties who have entered into defective riba transactions.

Marriage in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i presents the nikah contract's framework: conditions for validity (both parties must be free of impediments — mahram relationships, existing multiple marriages for a man), pillars (offer and acceptance before witnesses), and the factors that make a marriage void versus merely defective. The void marriage (no pillars) versus defective marriage (pillars present but some condition violated) distinction in Hanafi law has significant implications for the legal status of children born and for the parties' rights.

Divorce in Bada'i as-Sana'i presents the talaq's conditions, pillars, and effects systematically. Al-Kasani identifies the conditions for effective talaq (the husband must be adult, sane, and awake — not asleep — when pronouncing it), the forms of talaq (express words vs. implicit words, immediate vs. conditional), and the effects of each form on the 'iddah, revocability, and maintenance obligations.

Al-Kasani's systematic organization of commercial and family law in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i continues to make the work a valued reference in Hanafi legal education — a model of how systematic organization can enhance both the learnability and the applicability of an extensive legal tradition.
